_meta.source: Based on WHO guidelines, UpToDate, and standard clinical references. FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY.

fungal infection.dose: 150mg single dose (oral) or 200mg once daily for 7–14 days
fungal infection.reasoning: Fluconazole is the first-line treatment for most superficial fungal infections. It inhibits ergosterol synthesis disrupting fungal cell membrane integrity.
fungal infection.monitoring: Liver function tests with prolonged use

allergy.reasoning: Second-generation antihistamines are first-line for allergic symptoms. Cetirizine selectively blocks H1 receptors with minimal sedation.

gerd.dose: 20mg once daily before breakfast for 4–8 weeks
gerd.reasoning: PPIs are the most effective agents for acid suppression in GERD. Omeprazole irreversibly inhibits H+/K+ ATPase in gastric parietal cells.
gerd.monitoring: Magnesium levels with long-term use; B12 levels

chronic cholestasis.reasoning: UDCA replaces toxic bile acids, reduces hepatocyte injury, and improves bile flow in cholestatic liver disease.

drug reaction.reasoning: Immediate withdrawal of the offending drug is mandatory. Systemic corticosteroids are used for severe cutaneous reactions (e.g., DRESS, SJS).
drug reaction.monitoring: Blood glucose, electrolytes during steroid course

peptic ulcer disease.dose: Omeprazole 20mg BD + Amoxicillin 1g BD + Clarithromycin 500mg BD for 7–14 days (if H. pylori positive)
peptic ulcer disease.reasoning: H. pylori eradication is first-line for peptic ulcer disease. Triple therapy with PPI and two antibiotics achieves >85% eradication rates.
peptic ulcer disease.alternative: Bismuth quadruple therapy if clarithromycin-resistant region
peptic ulcer disease.monitoring: Repeat H. pylori test 4 weeks post-treatment

aids.reasoning: WHO-recommended first-line ART. Dolutegravir has high barrier to resistance, excellent tolerability, and once-daily dosing.
aids.monitoring: CD4 count, viral load every 6 months; renal function (TDF)

diabetes.dose: 500mg twice daily with meals, titrate to 1000mg twice daily
diabetes.reasoning: Metformin is first-line for Type 2 Diabetes (ADA guidelines). It reduces hepatic glucose production, improves insulin sensitivity, and is weight-neutral.
diabetes.monitoring: HbA1c every 3–6 months; eGFR annually; B12 levels

gastroenteritis.reasoning: Viral gastroenteritis is self-limiting. ORS prevents dehydration. Antibiotics not indicated unless bacterial cause confirmed (Salmonella, Shigella).
gastroenteritis.alternative: IV fluids if severe dehydration; Ciprofloxacin for bacterial GE

bronchial asthma.reasoning: GINA guidelines recommend ICS as first-line controller therapy. SABAs provide rapid bronchodilation for acute symptoms.
bronchial asthma.alternative: Montelukast as add-on; LABA + ICS if uncontrolled

hypertension.dose: 5mg once daily, titrate to 10mg if BP uncontrolled
hypertension.reasoning: CCBs and ACE inhibitors are first-line per JNC 8 / ESC guidelines. Amlodipine has long half-life, good tolerability, and reduces CV events.
hypertension.monitoring: BP every 4 weeks until target; electrolytes; renal function

migraine.dose: 50–100mg oral at onset; may repeat after 2h (max 200mg/24h)
migraine.reasoning: Triptans are the most effective abortive therapy for moderate-to-severe migraine. They cause cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit CGRP release.
migraine.alternative: Naproxen 500mg for mild attacks; Topiramate for prevention
migraine.monitoring: Cardiovascular status before use (Triptans contraindicated in CVD)

cervical spondylosis.dose: 400–600mg three times daily with food for 5–7 days
cervical spondylosis.reasoning: NSAIDs reduce inflammation and pain in cervical spondylosis. Physiotherapy is first-line non-pharmacological treatment.
cervical spondylosis.alternative: Paracetamol 1g TDS; Muscle relaxants (Cyclobenzaprine) for spasm
cervical spondylosis.monitoring: Renal function; GI symptoms with prolonged use

paralysis (brain hemorrhage).reasoning: Acute intracerebral hemorrhage requires blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation if applicable, and ICP management. Nimodipine prevents vasospasm in SAH.
paralysis (brain hemorrhage).alternative: Labetalol for acute BP control; Neurosurgical referral
paralysis (brain hemorrhage).monitoring: Neurological status; Glasgow Coma Scale; CT imaging

jaundice.first_line_drug: Treat underlying cause (e.g., UDCA for cholestasis, antivirals for hepatitis)
jaundice.reasoning: Jaundice is a symptom, not a diagnosis. Management requires identifying the underlying cause: pre-hepatic (haemolysis), hepatic (hepatitis, cirrhosis), or post-hepatic (biliary obstruction).

malaria.reasoning: WHO recommends ACTs as first-line for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. For severe malaria, IV Artesunate is preferred.
malaria.alternative: IV Artesunate for severe malaria; Chloroquine for P. vivax (if sensitive)
malaria.monitoring: Parasitaemia on blood film; CBC; renal function

chicken pox.dose: 800mg five times daily for 7 days (adult); 20mg/kg QID for 5 days (child)
chicken pox.reasoning: Aciclovir reduces duration and severity of varicella if started within 24h of rash onset. Most effective in immunocompromised patients.
chicken pox.alternative: Supportive care (antihistamines, calamine) for mild cases
chicken pox.monitoring: Secondary bacterial infection; renal function (IV dosing)

dengue.reasoning: No specific antiviral exists for dengue. NSAIDs and Aspirin are CONTRAINDICATED due to bleeding risk. Paracetamol controls fever safely.
dengue.alternative: IV fluid resuscitation for dengue haemorrhagic fever
dengue.monitoring: Platelet count daily; haematocrit; warning signs

typhoid.reasoning: Parenteral third-generation cephalosporins are preferred for severe typhoid. Oral alternatives: Azithromycin 1g loading then 500mg OD × 7 days.
typhoid.alternative: Azithromycin for uncomplicated typhoid; Ciprofloxacin where susceptibility confirmed
typhoid.monitoring: Blood cultures; fever curve; liver function

hepatitis a.reasoning: Hepatitis A is self-limiting in most cases. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs (alcohol, paracetamol excess). Vaccination prevents infection.

hepatitis b.reasoning: TDF is first-line for chronic Hepatitis B per EASL/AASLD guidelines. High barrier to resistance; can be used in pregnancy.
hepatitis b.alternative: Entecavir 0.5mg OD; Pegylated interferon for HBeAg+ with high ALT
hepatitis b.monitoring: HBV DNA every 3 months; LFTs; HBsAg/HBeAg status

hepatitis c.reasoning: DAAs achieve sustained virologic response (SVR) >95% in all genotypes. Pan-genotypic regimens eliminate need for genotyping.
hepatitis c.monitoring: HCV RNA at week 4 and 12 weeks post-treatment

hepatitis d.reasoning: Pegylated IFN-alfa is the only proven treatment for HDV. Bulevirtide (entry inhibitor) is newly approved in Europe.
hepatitis d.monitoring: HDV RNA; ALT; neuropsychiatric side effects

hepatitis e.dose: No specific antiviral for immunocompetent patients
hepatitis e.reasoning: Hepatitis E is self-limiting in immunocompetent patients. Ribavirin is used in immunocompromised/transplant patients.
hepatitis e.monitoring: LFTs; INR; extra care in pregnancy (high mortality)

alcoholic hepatitis.dose: 40mg once daily for 28 days (Maddrey Discriminant Function >32)
alcoholic hepatitis.reasoning: Prednisolone reduces short-term mortality in severe alcoholic hepatitis. Calculate Maddrey DF first; contraindicated if active infection or GI bleed.
alcoholic hepatitis.monitoring: Lille score at day 7; blood glucose; infection surveillance

tuberculosis.first_line_drug: HRZE regimen — Isoniazid + Rifampicin + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol
tuberculosis.dose: Intensive phase: 2 months HRZE; Continuation phase: 4 months HR
tuberculosis.reasoning: Standard 6-month DOTS regimen per WHO guidelines. Never use monotherapy — combination prevents resistance. Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) co-administered to prevent INH neuropathy.
tuberculosis.monitoring: LFTs monthly; visual acuity (Ethambutol); sputum AFB monthly

common cold.first_line_drug: Supportive care — Paracetamol + Nasal saline + Fluids
common cold.reasoning: Common cold is self-limiting (rhinovirus). Antibiotics are not indicated. Zinc lozenges may reduce duration if started within 24h.
common cold.alternative: Pseudoephedrine for congestion; Dextromethorphan for cough

pneumonia.dose: 1g three times daily for 5–7 days (community-acquired, outpatient)
pneumonia.reasoning: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common CAP pathogen. Amoxicillin covers typical organisms. Azithromycin added for atypical cover if needed.
pneumonia.alternative: Azithromycin + Ceftriaxone for hospitalised patients; Levofloxacin as monotherapy
pneumonia.monitoring: Oxygen saturation; CXR at 6 weeks; blood cultures before antibiotics

dimorphic hemorrhoids (piles).dose: Apply to affected area 2–4 times daily for 5–7 days
dimorphic hemorrhoids (piles).reasoning: First-line for symptomatic relief. High-fibre diet and adequate hydration are essential non-pharmacological interventions.
dimorphic hemorrhoids (piles).alternative: Rubber band ligation for grade II–III; Haemorrhoidectomy for severe cases
dimorphic hemorrhoids (piles).monitoring: Duration of topical steroid use; surgical referral criteria

heart attack.dose: Aspirin 300mg loading + Clopidogrel 300mg loading; Atorvastatin 80mg OD
heart attack.reasoning: STEMI management: immediate aspirin, dual antiplatelet therapy, statin, and reperfusion strategy within 90 minutes (primary PCI preferred).
heart attack.alternative: Thrombolysis (Alteplase) if PCI unavailable within 120 min
heart attack.monitoring: Cardiac biomarkers (Troponin); ECG monitoring; LVEF echocardiography

varicose veins.dose: Diosmin 1000mg once daily; Compression class II (23–32 mmHg)
varicose veins.reasoning: Compression reduces venous hypertension. Diosmin improves venous tone and reduces inflammation. Endovenous laser ablation for significant disease.

hypothyroidism.dose: 1.6 mcg/kg/day, starting at 25–50mcg OD and titrating every 4–6 weeks
hypothyroidism.reasoning: Levothyroxine is the gold standard. Dose titrated to achieve TSH in normal range (0.4–4.0 mIU/L). Take 30–60 min before food.
hypothyroidism.alternative: Add T3 (Liothyronine) for persistent symptoms despite normal TSH
hypothyroidism.monitoring: TSH every 4–6 weeks until stable; then annually; lipid profile

hyperthyroidism.dose: 20–40mg once daily initially; reduce to 5–15mg maintenance
hyperthyroidism.reasoning: Carbimazole blocks thyroid peroxidase, reducing T3/T4 synthesis. Used for Graves' disease. Propranolol added for symptomatic control (tachycardia, tremor).

hypoglycemia.first_line_drug: Oral glucose (if conscious) or Glucagon / IV Dextrose (if unconscious)
hypoglycemia.dose: 15–20g fast-acting glucose (glucose tablets or juice); IV 50ml of 50% dextrose
hypoglycemia.reasoning: Immediate glucose correction is essential. Follow 15-15 rule: 15g glucose, recheck BG after 15 minutes, repeat if still low.
hypoglycemia.alternative: IM Glucagon 1mg if IV access not available
hypoglycemia.monitoring: Blood glucose every 15 minutes until normalised

osteoarthritis.reasoning: Paracetamol is preferred first-line as NSAIDs carry GI/cardiovascular risks. Topical diclofenac is effective for knee OA with reduced systemic risk.
osteoarthritis.alternative: Topical Diclofenac gel; Oral NSAIDs if insufficient; Intra-articular corticosteroid
osteoarthritis.monitoring: Pain VAS score; NSAID use — GI protection with PPI

arthritis.dose: 7.5–25mg once weekly (oral or SC) + Folic acid 5mg/week
arthritis.reasoning: Methotrexate is the anchor DMARD for RA per ACR/EULAR guidelines. Folic acid supplementation reduces toxicity. Folic acid must always be co-prescribed.
arthritis.alternative: Hydroxychloroquine for mild RA; Biologic DMARDs (anti-TNF) for refractory disease
arthritis.monitoring: CBC, LFTs every 4–8 weeks; annual chest X-ray

paroxysmal positional vertigo.reasoning: The Epley manoeuvre is curative in >80% of BPPV cases. Betahistine improves inner ear blood flow and reduces vestibular neurotransmission.

acne.dose: Benzoyl peroxide 5% gel OD; Clindamycin 1% gel BD
acne.reasoning: Combination therapy with benzoyl peroxide and antibiotic is more effective than monotherapy and reduces antibiotic resistance.
acne.alternative: Oral Doxycycline 100mg OD for moderate-severe; Isotretinoin for severe/nodular acne
acne.monitoring: Skin response at 8–10 weeks; Isotretinoin liver and lipid monitoring

urinary tract infection.dose: 100mg modified-release twice daily for 5 days (uncomplicated UTI)
urinary tract infection.reasoning: Nitrofurantoin is first-line for uncomplicated lower UTI (NICE guidelines). Concentrates in urine. Avoid if eGFR <45 or in late pregnancy.
urinary tract infection.alternative: Trimethoprim 200mg BD × 7 days; Fosfomycin 3g single dose
urinary tract infection.monitoring: Urine culture and sensitivity; symptoms at day 3

psoriasis.dose: Apply once daily for up to 4 weeks; max 100g/week
psoriasis.reasoning: Combination calcipotriol + corticosteroid is superior to either agent alone. Calcipotriol slows keratinocyte proliferation; corticosteroid reduces inflammation.
psoriasis.alternative: Methotrexate for moderate–severe psoriasis; Biologics (Adalimumab, Secukinumab) for severe disease
psoriasis.monitoring: PASI score; skin atrophy with prolonged topical steroid use

impetigo.dose: Apply to affected area 3 times daily for 5–7 days
impetigo.reasoning: Non-bullous impetigo (S. aureus, S. pyogenes) responds well to topical fusidic acid. Oral antibiotics for extensive/bullous disease.
impetigo.alternative: Topical Mupirocin; Oral Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS for extensive impetigo
impetigo.monitoring: Lesion resolution at 5–7 days; glomerulonephritis risk (post-streptococcal)

gastroenteritis (acute).dose: ORS 10ml/kg after each stool; Zinc 20mg OD for 10–14 days (children)
gastroenteritis (acute).reasoning: WHO recommends ORS and zinc for childhood gastroenteritis. Zinc reduces duration and severity. Probiotics (Lactobacillus) may reduce duration.
gastroenteritis (acute).alternative: Azithromycin for confirmed bacterial GE; Metronidazole for Giardia
gastroenteritis (acute).monitoring: Hydration status; weight; signs of sepsis
